chapter: Ch 2 · Framework for Preparation and Presentation of Financial Statements

questions.stem: Where a provision of the Framework conflicts with an Accounting Standard:

options.text: The preparer may follow either
options.explanation: No election exists; the Standard governs.

options.text: The Framework prevails, being the conceptual foundation
options.explanation: Conceptual seniority does not confer legal priority — the Standard wins.

options.text: The matter is referred to the NFRA for resolution
options.explanation: No referral mechanism is needed — the hierarchy is settled in the Framework itself.

options.text: The Accounting Standard prevails — the Framework is not itself a standard
options.explanation: The Framework guides and underlies the standards but yields to them in any conflict.

readLink.label: Read: What the Framework is for →

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 2 §3 — status and scope of the Framework

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a purpose the Framework serves?

options.text: Assisting standard-setters in developing and reviewing Accounting Standards
options.explanation: Guiding the ASB is a core stated purpose.

options.text: Helping preparers deal with topics not yet covered by any standard
options.explanation: Filling gaps where no standard exists is a stated purpose.

options.text: Helping auditors form an opinion on whether statements conform with standards
options.explanation: Assisting auditors is a stated purpose.

options.text: Prescribing the tax treatment of business income
options.explanation: Tax computation belongs to tax law — the Framework serves financial reporting only.

readLink.label: Read: What the Framework is for →

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 2 §2 — purpose of the Framework

questions.stem: The three FUNDAMENTAL accounting assumptions named by the Framework are:

options.text: Going concern, accrual and consistency
options.explanation: The canonical trio — presumed followed unless a departure is disclosed.

options.text: Entity, money measurement and periodicity
options.explanation: Those are traditional accounting concepts, not the Framework's fundamental assumptions.

options.text: Going concern, prudence and matching
options.explanation: Prudence and matching are principles, not the named fundamental assumptions.

options.text: Prudence, materiality and substance over form
options.explanation: Those are qualitative characteristics/sub-traits of reliability, not the fundamental assumptions.

questions.stem: A company's financial statements say NOTHING about the fundamental accounting assumptions. A reader should conclude that:

options.text: The company has abandoned the going concern basis
options.explanation: Abandoning going concern is exactly what WOULD require disclosure.

options.text: The auditor must qualify the report
options.explanation: There is nothing to qualify — silence is the compliant position.

options.text: The statements are deficient for want of a compliance note
options.explanation: No compliance note is required when the assumptions are followed.

options.text: All three assumptions have been followed — disclosure is required only on departure
options.explanation: Silence means compliance; only NOT following a fundamental assumption demands specific disclosure.

questions.stem: The going concern assumption means the enterprise:

options.text: Is guaranteed to remain profitable indefinitely
options.explanation: Continuity, not profitability, is the assumption — loss-makers can be going concerns.

options.text: Will never be sold or merged
options.explanation: Sale or merger of a continuing business does not offend the assumption.

options.text: Will continue in operation for the foreseeable future, with neither intention nor need to materially curtail operations
options.explanation: That continuity is what justifies cost-based carrying amounts and orderly provision for liabilities.

options.text: Values all assets at net realisable value
options.explanation: NRV valuation is the DEPARTURE basis — used precisely when going concern fails, with disclosure.

questions.stem: The consistency assumption permits a change in accounting policy:

options.text: Never — once chosen, a policy is permanent
options.explanation: Consistency is not permanence; required or better policies may replace old ones.

options.text: Whenever management prefers a higher reported profit
options.explanation: Profit-steering is precisely the abuse consistency exists to prevent.

options.text: Only at intervals of five years
options.explanation: No time-lock exists in the assumption.

options.text: Where a statute or an Accounting Standard requires it, or where the change presents the statements more appropriately
options.explanation: Consistency means no ARBITRARY change — justified changes with disclosure are allowed.

questions.stem: Under the accrual assumption, a transaction is recognised:

options.text: At the earliest of invoice date and payment date, always
options.explanation: The test is occurrence and the period to which the item relates, not a date race.

options.text: When it occurs, in the period to which it relates — not when cash is received or paid
options.explanation: Accrual divorces recognition from cash movement.

options.text: Only when the resulting cash is actually received or paid
options.explanation: That is CASH-basis accounting, which the assumption rejects.

options.text: When the auditor approves the entry
options.explanation: Audit timing has nothing to do with recognition.

questions.stem: The four PRINCIPAL qualitative characteristics of financial information are:

options.text: Accuracy, timeliness, brevity and precision
options.explanation: None of these is the Framework's named quartet.

options.text: Understandability, relevance, reliability and comparability
options.explanation: The four pillars; materiality lives inside relevance, and faithful representation, substance over form, neutrality, prudence and completeness inside reliability.

options.text: Recognition, measurement, presentation and disclosure
options.explanation: Those are the four ASPECTS a standard regulates, not qualities of information.

options.text: Going concern, accrual, consistency and prudence
options.explanation: The first three are fundamental ASSUMPTIONS, not qualitative characteristics.

questions.stem: An item is MATERIAL when:

options.text: It appears on the face of the balance sheet rather than the notes
options.explanation: Placement follows materiality; it does not create it.

options.text: The auditor selects it for sampling
options.explanation: Audit sampling choices do not define materiality.

options.text: Its misstatement or omission could influence the economic decisions of users — judged by size and nature, not a fixed percentage
options.explanation: Materiality is a user-impact threshold tied to relevance; a small-rupee item can be material by nature.

options.text: It exceeds 5% of turnover in every case
options.explanation: No universal percentage exists — the test is influence on decisions.

questions.stem: A buyer under a hire-purchase agreement records the asset in its books although legal title remains with the seller until the last instalment. This applies the principle of:

options.text: Substance over form
options.explanation: Economic reality (the buyer enjoys and controls the asset's benefits) overrides the legal packaging.

options.text: Going concern
options.explanation: Continuity of operations is not what puts the HP asset on the buyer's books.

options.text: Prudence
options.explanation: Prudence concerns caution about profits and losses, not legal-vs-economic characterisation.

options.text: Materiality
options.explanation: Materiality is a significance threshold, not a characterisation rule.

questions.stem: Prudence requires that an enterprise:

options.text: Understates profits every year as a safety margin
options.explanation: Systematic understatement is bias — the very thing neutrality forbids.

options.text: Creates secret reserves in good years to smooth bad ones
options.explanation: Hidden reserves are the classic ABUSE of prudence, not its requirement.

options.text: Recognises expected gains as soon as they become likely
options.explanation: Anticipating unrealised profits is exactly what prudence prevents.

options.text: Does not anticipate profits and provides for all known losses — without deliberately understating assets or creating hidden reserves
options.explanation: Caution within limits: deliberate understatement would breach neutrality and reliability.

questions.stem: Information in financial statements is NEUTRAL when it:

options.text: Is free from bias — not selected or presented to steer users toward a predetermined conclusion
options.explanation: Neutrality is the no-thumb-on-the-scale component of reliability.

options.text: Avoids all estimates and judgements
options.explanation: Estimates are unavoidable; neutrality governs how they are made, not whether.

options.text: Reports identical profit each year
options.explanation: Smoothed results would be biased, not neutral.

options.text: Is reviewed by an independent director
options.explanation: Governance review does not define the characteristic.

questions.stem: Per the Framework's position, financial statements ordinarily convey a TRUE AND FAIR view when they:

options.text: Are certified by two independent valuers
options.explanation: Valuation certificates are no substitute for standards compliance.

options.text: Match the tax return figures exactly
options.explanation: Tax and books legitimately diverge — matching them proves nothing.

options.text: Apply the principal qualitative characteristics and the applicable accounting standards
options.explanation: Quality plus compliance is what ordinarily delivers the true and fair view.

options.text: Disclose every transaction individually
options.explanation: Aggregation is normal; item-by-item listing is neither required nor useful.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 2 §8 — true and fair view

questions.stem: The Framework defines an ASSET as:

options.text: A resource controlled by the enterprise as a result of past events, from which future economic benefits are expected to flow
options.explanation: Control (not ownership), a past event, and expected future benefits — all three limbs matter.

options.text: Expenditure carried forward to future periods
options.explanation: Deferred expenditure is not the definition — future benefit from a controlled resource is.

options.text: Anything with a realisable market value
options.explanation: Market value alone does not make a resource the enterprise's asset.

options.text: Any property legally owned by the enterprise
options.explanation: Ownership is neither necessary (leased/HP assets) nor sufficient — control and future benefits are the tests.

questions.stem: A LIABILITY, per the Framework, is:

options.text: Any expected future commitment, whether or not an obligation exists today
options.explanation: A mere future intention (e.g. planned repairs) is not a PRESENT obligation.

options.text: The residual interest in the assets of the enterprise
options.explanation: The residual is EQUITY, not a liability.

options.text: A present obligation arising from past events, whose settlement is expected to cause an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits
options.explanation: Present obligation + past event + expected outflow — the three limbs.

options.text: Only amounts legally enforceable in court
options.explanation: Constructive obligations from practice or policy also qualify — legal enforceability is not the boundary.

questions.stem: EQUITY is defined in the Framework as:

options.text: The market capitalisation of the company
options.explanation: Market value of shares is a stock-exchange number, not the accounting element.

options.text: The residual interest in the assets of the enterprise after deducting all its liabilities
options.explanation: Equity is never defined independently — it is what remains.

options.text: Assets plus liabilities
options.explanation: Equity is assets MINUS liabilities.

options.text: The paid-up share capital only
options.explanation: Reserves and surplus are equally part of the residual.

questions.stem: Under the Framework's element definitions:

options.text: Gains are equity contributions, not income
options.explanation: Owner CONTRIBUTIONS are excluded from income; gains are squarely inside it.

options.text: Income embraces both revenue (ordinary activities) and gains; expenses embrace ordinary expenses and losses
options.explanation: Both elements are defined broadly, with owner contributions/distributions excluded.

options.text: Revenue and gains are separate elements of financial statements
options.explanation: The Framework has five elements — revenue and gains are both parts of the single element, income.

options.text: Losses bypass the P&L and go straight to reserves
options.explanation: Losses are expenses; only owner distributions bypass income/expense.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 2 §9 — elements: income and expenses

questions.stem: An element is RECOGNISED — taken onto the balance sheet or into the statement of profit and loss — only when:

options.text: Either probability or reliable measurement is satisfied
options.explanation: One without the other leaves the item to note disclosure at most.

options.text: It is probable that future economic benefits will flow to or from the enterprise, AND the item's cost or value can be measured reliably
options.explanation: Both tests together — probability and reliable measurement.

options.text: The board of directors approves the item
options.explanation: Board approval is governance, not a recognition criterion.

options.text: The transaction has been settled in cash
options.explanation: Cash settlement is a cash-basis idea; accrual recognition needs no settlement.

questions.stem: The four measurement bases recognised by the Framework are:

options.text: Cost model, revaluation model, fair value model and equity method
options.explanation: Those are standard-level accounting models, not the Framework's four bases.

options.text: Historical cost, replacement cost, scrap value and face value
options.explanation: Scrap and face value are not Framework bases.

options.text: Historical cost, current cost, realisable (settlement) value and present value
options.explanation: Usable in varying combinations, with historical cost dominant under the ASs.

options.text: Cost, market, fair and book value
options.explanation: Not the Framework's list.

questions.stem: Indian Accounting Standards (AS) predominantly measure items at:

options.text: Historical cost, with pointed exceptions such as inventory at the lower of cost and net realisable value
options.explanation: Historical cost is the workhorse; other bases appear where a standard directs.

options.text: Current cost throughout
options.explanation: Current-cost accounting was never the AS default.

options.text: Realisable value throughout
options.explanation: Wholesale NRV accounting is the liquidation basis, not the AS norm.

options.text: Present value throughout
options.explanation: Discounting appears selectively, not as the default.

questions.stem: Closing assets ₹9,00,000; closing liabilities ₹3,40,000; opening assets ₹7,00,000; opening liabilities ₹2,80,000; capital introduced ₹50,000; drawings ₹90,000. Using P = (CA − CL) − (OA − OL) − C + D, profit for the year is:

options.explanation: ₹2,20,000 adds the capital introduced instead of deducting it (and mishandles drawings).
₹1,00,000 subtracts the drawings instead of adding them back.
₹1,40,000 nets both capital introduced AND drawings the wrong way — drawings are ADDED back, capital introduced deducted.

questions.stem: Opening equity at historical cost is ₹4,00,000. The general price index moved from 100 (opening) to 115 (closing). Under financial capital maintenance at current purchasing power, the capital to be maintained is:

options.explanation: ₹3,47,826 divides by the index ratio instead of multiplying.
₹4,15,000 adds 15 points as rupees instead of applying the ratio 115/100.
4,00,000 × 115/100 = ₹4,60,000 — the opening equity restated to closing prices.
₹4,00,000 is the HISTORICAL-cost capital; CPP restates it by the index.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 2 §11 — financial capital maintenance at CPP

questions.stem: A machine is expected to generate a single net cash inflow of ₹1,21,000 two years from now. At a 10% discount rate, its PRESENT VALUE is:

options.explanation: ₹1,10,000 discounts only one year.
₹96,800 applies straight-line 10% for two years (deducting 20% of the future sum) instead of compounding.
₹1,21,000 is the undiscounted future amount.

questions.stem: Inventory was acquired at a historical cost of ₹80,000. At the year end its net realisable value is ₹72,000. Applying the standard AS treatment, it is carried at:

options.explanation: Lower of cost (₹80,000) and NRV (₹72,000) — the classic mixed-measurement exception to pure historical cost.
₹8,000 is the write-DOWN, not the carrying amount.
₹80,000 ignores the NRV fall — the lower-of rule bites.
₹76,000 averages the two figures; no averaging exists in the rule.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 2 §10 — inventory at lower of cost and NRV

questions.stem: The Framework addresses financial statements prepared for:

options.text: A wide range of external users — investors, employees, lenders, suppliers, customers, government and the public
options.explanation: General-purpose external reporting is the Framework's field.

options.text: Credit rating agencies alone
options.explanation: Rating agencies are just one class of user.

options.text: Management's internal decision-making only
options.explanation: Internal reporting is management accounting's territory.

options.text: The income-tax department exclusively
options.explanation: Tax authorities are one user among many, and tax computation follows its own law.

readLink.label: Read: What the Framework is for →

questions.stem: Under PHYSICAL capital maintenance, profit is earned only when:

options.text: Cash at bank has increased during the year
options.explanation: Cash movement is no capital-maintenance concept at all.

options.text: Closing equity exceeds opening equity restated by a general price index
options.explanation: Index restatement is financial capital maintenance at current purchasing power.

options.text: The enterprise's operating capability at the end of the period exceeds that at the start — i.e. after providing for replacement of assets consumed at current cost
options.explanation: Physical maintenance protects productive capacity, not rupee amounts.

options.text: Closing equity at historical cost exceeds opening equity
options.explanation: That is FINANCIAL capital maintenance at historical cost.

questions.topic: Ch 2 · Framework · Case: Panna Gems — assumptions under strain
questions.case: Panna Gems Ltd is finalising its accounts. Four situations arise: (i) a major customer's insolvency after year end has management planning to halve operations and sell two of three workshops — yet the draft accounts carry all assets at cost with no comment; (ii) the accounts say nothing anywhere about going concern, accrual or consistency, though all three were followed; (iii) the company switched inventory valuation from FIFO to weighted average because the new AS-permitted method presents results more appropriately, and disclosed the change; (iv) commission income due for March has been booked although the cash arrived in April.

questions.stem: Situation (i) — planned major curtailment with assets still at cost, undisclosed — involves:

options.text: A departure from the going concern assumption that MUST be disclosed, with the alternative basis stated
options.explanation: Intention to materially curtail operations undermines going concern; the basis change and disclosure are mandatory.

options.text: Only a consistency problem
options.explanation: The assumption at stake is going concern, not consistency.

options.text: A breach of the accrual assumption
options.explanation: Accrual is untouched — the failure is continuity and disclosure.

options.text: No issue, since insolvency occurred after the year end
options.explanation: The curtailment DECISION drives the basis of preparation regardless of the trigger's timing.

questions.stem: Situation (ii) — total silence about the three assumptions, all followed — is:

options.text: Acceptable only if the auditor certifies the assumptions separately
options.explanation: No separate certification exists for the assumptions.

options.text: A reportable deficiency
options.explanation: Nothing is deficient; no positive statement of compliance is required.

options.text: Perfectly compliant: disclosure is needed only when a fundamental assumption is NOT followed
options.explanation: Silence signals compliance — the Framework's presumption does the work.

options.text: A breach of the completeness characteristic
options.explanation: Completeness does not require stating what is presumed.

questions.stem: Situation (iii) — the disclosed FIFO → weighted average change — is:

options.text: Permissible only with NFRA approval
options.explanation: No regulator pre-approves policy changes.

options.text: A going concern issue
options.explanation: Inventory method choice has nothing to do with continuity.

options.text: A violation of consistency in every case
options.explanation: Consistency has recognised gateways — statute, standard, or better presentation.

options.text: Consistent with the consistency assumption: a change for more appropriate presentation, made with disclosure
options.explanation: Consistency forbids arbitrary change, not justified and disclosed improvement.

questions.stem: Situation (iv) — March commission booked in March, received in April — applies:

options.text: The prudence principle, incorrectly
options.explanation: Nothing imprudent here — the income was earned and measurable.

options.text: The materiality threshold
options.explanation: Materiality is not what places the income in March.

options.text: The accrual assumption, correctly
options.explanation: Recognition follows occurrence and the period to which the income relates, not the cash date.

options.text: Cash-basis accounting
options.explanation: Cash basis would delay the income to April — the OPPOSITE of what was done.

questions.topic: Ch 2 · Framework · Case: Bhadra Motors — naming the characteristic
questions.case: Identify the qualitative characteristic (or its breach) in each situation at Bhadra Motors Ltd: (i) vehicles supplied to dealers on sale-or-return terms are excluded from sales until the dealers actually sell them, although invoices were raised; (ii) the CFO wants to create an undisclosed provision in a record year 'to smooth next year's numbers'; (iii) a ₹700 stationery purchase was expensed rather than capitalised and depreciated over its two-year useful life; (iv) the company presents five-year comparative summaries using uniform policies.

questions.stem: Situation (i) — sale-or-return stock excluded from sales despite invoicing — applies:

options.text: Going concern
options.explanation: Continuity is irrelevant to revenue characterisation.

options.text: Understandability
options.explanation: Comprehension is not what excludes the invoiced goods from sales.

options.text: Materiality
options.explanation: The issue is characterisation of the transaction, not its size.

options.text: Substance over form: no sale has occurred in economic reality until the dealer sells
options.explanation: The invoice (form) yields to the retained risk and control (substance).

questions.stem: Situation (ii) — the undisclosed profit-smoothing provision — would breach:

options.text: The accrual assumption
options.explanation: Accrual timing is not the issue — truthfulness is.

options.text: Neutrality (and misuse prudence): deliberate understatement and hidden reserves bias the statements
options.explanation: Prudence never licenses secret reserves — that is bias, the enemy of neutrality and reliability.

options.text: Nothing — prudence encourages extra provisions
options.explanation: Prudence provides for KNOWN losses; smoothing provisions fabricate them.

options.text: Only the consistency assumption
options.explanation: The vice is bias, not policy change.

questions.stem: Situation (iii) — expensing the ₹700 stationery item outright — is justified by:

options.text: Nothing — it violates the matching principle
options.explanation: Materiality expressly tolerates such shortcuts.

options.text: Substance over form
options.explanation: No legal-vs-economic divergence exists here.

options.text: Materiality: the amount is too small to influence any user's decision, so strict capitalisation is not demanded
options.explanation: Immaterial items may be dealt with expediently — the cost of precision would exceed its value.

options.text: Prudence: expensing is always safer
options.explanation: The justification is insignificance, not caution.

questions.stem: Situation (iv) — five-year summaries on uniform policies — chiefly serves:

options.text: Comparability, across periods
options.explanation: Uniform policies over time are exactly what inter-period comparability demands.

options.text: Neutrality
options.explanation: Freedom from bias is not the quality showcased by uniform time series.

options.text: Faithful representation only
options.explanation: Faithfulness matters, but the five-year uniform view is comparability's showcase.

options.text: Prudence
options.explanation: Caution about profits has nothing to do with multi-year summaries.

questions.topic: Ch 2 · Framework · Case: Tawa Traders — one year, three profit verdicts
questions.case: Tawa Traders commenced the year with capital of ₹20,000, held entirely as 5,000 units of a single product bought at ₹4 per unit. During the year all 5,000 units were sold at ₹5 per unit (₹25,000) and the proprietor withdrew ₹3,000; the closing balance is all cash, ₹22,000. At the year end the supplier's price for the same product is ₹4.60 per unit, and the general price index has risen from 100 to 110.

questions.stem: Profit under financial capital maintenance at HISTORICAL COST is:

options.explanation: ₹3,000 is the CPP profit (25,000 − 22,000), not the historical-cost figure.
₹2,000 is the RETAINED profit after drawings (5,000 − 3,000), not the profit.
Sales 25,000 − historical capital 20,000 = ₹5,000.
₹25,000 is gross sales — capital must first be recovered.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 2 §11 — financial capital maintenance at historical cost

questions.stem: The capital to be maintained under financial capital maintenance at CURRENT PURCHASING POWER is:

options.explanation: ₹21,000 applies half the index rise; the full 10% applies.
20,000 × 110/100 = ₹22,000 — opening equity restated to closing general prices.
₹23,000 uses the SPECIFIC replacement price (physical maintenance), not the general index.
₹20,000 is the unrestated historical capital.

questions.stem: Profit under PHYSICAL capital maintenance is:

options.explanation: ₹3,000 is the CPP profit, using the general index instead of the specific price.
₹5,000 is the historical-cost profit — physical maintenance charges replacement cost.

options.text: Nil
options.explanation: Sales exceeded the replacement cost of capacity by ₹2,000 — profit is positive before drawings.

questions.stem: After the ₹3,000 drawing, retained profit under PHYSICAL capital maintenance is:

options.text: (₹1,000) — operating capability has NOT been maintained
options.explanation: Profit 2,000 − drawings 3,000 = −₹1,000; ₹1,000 of the drawing was capital consumed, not income.

options.text: ₹1,000 positive
options.explanation: The sign is negative — drawings exceeded physical-maintenance profit.

options.text: Nil — exactly maintained
options.explanation: Nil is the CPP verdict (3,000 − 3,000); the physical test fails by ₹1,000.

options.text: ₹2,000 — maintained comfortably
options.explanation: ₹2,000 ignores the drawing entirely.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 2 §11 — retained profit under physical maintenance

questions.stem: That the SAME year passes the historical-cost test, exactly meets the CPP test, and fails the physical test demonstrates that:

options.text: General price indices always overstate inflation
options.explanation: Here the specific price (15%) outran the index (10%) — neither always leads.

options.text: Physical maintenance is legally mandatory in India
options.explanation: Indian practice runs on financial capital maintenance at historical cost; physical is a concept, not a mandate.

options.text: The choice of capital-maintenance concept determines what counts as distributable profit
options.explanation: Profit is a function of the capital concept — the chapter's central lesson.

options.text: The trader kept defective books
options.explanation: The books are identical in all three cases; only the capital yardstick changed.

questions.stem: State the purposes of the Framework and its status vis-à-vis Accounting Standards. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Assists ASB in developing/reviewing standards; preparers in applying standards and treating uncovered topics; auditors in forming opinions; users in interpretation
The Framework is NOT an Accounting Standard and does not override any specific standard
In conflict, the Accounting Standard prevails over the Framework

questions.stem: Name and explain the fundamental accounting assumptions. What disclosure do they require? (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Going concern: continued operation in the foreseeable future; no intention/need to materially curtail; departure basis (e.g. NRV) must be disclosed
Accrual: effects recognised when they occur, recorded in the periods to which they relate, regardless of cash timing
Consistency: uniform policies period to period; change allowed if required by statute/AS or for more appropriate presentation
Disclosure: none needed if followed; a departure from any assumption must be specifically disclosed

questions.stem: Discuss the qualitative characteristics that make financial information useful. (6 marks)

skeleton.point: Understandability: comprehensible to reasonably knowledgeable, diligent users
Relevance: capable of influencing decisions; materiality as its threshold (size AND nature)
Reliability: faithful representation; substance over form; neutrality; prudence (no anticipated profits, all known losses — no hidden reserves); completeness
Comparability: across periods and enterprises; consistent policies and disclosure make it possible

questions.stem: Define the five elements of financial statements per the Framework. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Asset: resource controlled by the enterprise as a result of past events from which future economic benefits are expected to flow
Liability: present obligation from past events, settlement expected to cause outflow of resources embodying economic benefits
Equity: residual interest in assets after deducting all liabilities
Income: increases in economic benefits (inflows/enhancements of assets, decreases of liabilities) increasing equity, other than owner contributions — includes revenue and gains
Expense: decreases in economic benefits decreasing equity, other than owner distributions — includes losses

questions.stem: When is an element recognised in financial statements? What happens to items failing the criteria? (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Recognition requires BOTH: probable inflow/outflow of future economic benefits, and reliable measurement of cost or value
Items failing either test are not recognised on the face; they may still merit disclosure in the notes (e.g. contingencies)

questions.stem: Explain the four measurement bases recognised by the Framework, with an example of mixed measurement in Indian practice. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Historical cost: amount paid / consideration at acquisition; obligations at proceeds received or amounts expected to be paid
Current cost: cost to acquire the equivalent asset now; undiscounted settlement amount now
Realisable (settlement) value: orderly-disposal proceeds now; undiscounted expected settlement
Present value: discounted future net cash flows
AS practice largely historical cost; e.g. inventory acquired at cost but carried at lower of cost and NRV at year end

questions.stem: Explain the three bases for checking capital maintenance, illustrating how the same results can yield different profit figures. (6 marks)

skeleton.point: Setting: P = (CA−CL) − (OA−OL) − C + D; retained profit (P−D) must be non-negative for capital to be maintained
Financial at historical cost: equity at historical rupees; adequate only with stable prices
Financial at current purchasing power: opening equity restated by general price index to closing prices; ignores asset-specific price moves
Physical: maintain operating capability — profit only after providing replacement cost of assets consumed
Illustration: one trading year can pass historical, just meet CPP and fail physical when specific prices outrun the index

questions.topic: Ch 2 · Framework · True and fair; users
questions.stem: Who are the users of financial statements, and how do financial statements achieve a true and fair view? (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Users: investors (and potential investors), employees, lenders, suppliers and trade creditors, customers, government and agencies, the public
Objective: information on financial position, performance and cash flows useful for economic decisions
True and fair view is ordinarily achieved by applying the principal qualitative characteristics and the applicable accounting standards
